name: History of Chili, with Original Recipe
recipeIngredient: 1 teaspoon Cayenne pepper (more or less as desired)
recipeInstructions:
The first "chili" recipes appeared in West Texas at the turn of the century. They may have originated from old Mexican recipes, but since most cowboys couldn't read or cook well, chili probably started due to the availability of spices and ingredients in the area. Many modern ingredients were unavailable back then. Most chili consisted of beef, cumin, pepper, sugar, paprika, garlic, and masa for thickening. Tomatoes were seasonal and usually not available. Chili powder was not manufactured at that time; they used dried chilies (Spanish for peppers). Most West Texas cowboys were Mexican. Original chili rarely included beans due to the long soaking and cooking time required.
1. In a large skillet, sauté meat in oil until browned.
2. Add water and simmer for 1½ to 2 hours.
3. In a small bowl, mix chili powder, salt, garlic, cumin, oregano, cayenne pepper, black pepper, sugar, and paprika.
4. Gradually add the spice mixture to the skillet, stirring constantly.
5. Simmer for an additional 30 minutes; longer cooking may cause spices to lose flavor.
6. In a small bowl, mix flour and masa.
7. Whisk in the flour and masa mixture into the chili, stirring continuously to prevent sticking.
8. Return the mixture to a simmer until thickened.
9. Remove from heat and serve over beans, rice, hot dogs, enchiladas, burritos, or enjoy plain. Cheese (cheddar, longhorn, queso blanco, etc.) pairs well on top.
Note: The original recipe lacks tomatoes and beans. Give it a try!
